payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /worksp/monitor/app/docs/development.md
@@
 The validator tests are the most important — run them whenever you add or reclassify
 a command pattern:
 
 ```sh
 cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
 ```
 
+The NAS API validator uses Go's `regexp` package, which is RE2 syntax. Do not use
+PCRE/JavaScript-only features such as lookahead, lookbehind, or backreferences in
+`regexp.MustCompile` patterns. An invalid `MustCompile` pattern panics during
+package initialization and crash-loops `synology-monitor-nas-api` on both NASes
+after Watchtower pulls the image. If the local machine lacks Go, run the validator
+tests in Docker:
+
+```sh
+docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
+  go test ./internal/validator/...
+```
+
 ### TypeScript
@@
 Always add a test in `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go`:
 
 ```go
 {name: "my_new_tool tier2", command: "synopkg restart MyPackage", wantTier: 2},
 ```
 
+Regex rule: `apps/nas-api` is Go, so `regexp.MustCompile` patterns must be valid
+Go/RE2 regular expressions. Do not use negative lookahead like `(?!...)` to make
+exceptions. Express the positive match instead, or write a small Go helper and test
+both the matching and exception cases.
+
 Run:
 ```sh
 cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
 ```
@@
 ### NAS API not responding
 
 1. Check `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api` on the NAS.
 2. Verify Tailscale is connected: the web app reaches NAS API over Tailscale IPs
    (`100.107.131.35:7734` / `100.107.131.36:7734`).
 3. Health check: `GET http://<nas-tailscale-ip>:7734/health` returns `{"status":"ok"}`.
    This endpoint requires no auth.
 4. A silent 403 on every request usually means the `NAS_API_SECRET` in Coolify does
    not match `NAS_API_SECRET` in the NAS `.env` — credential parity mismatch.
+5. `ECONNREFUSED` means the NAS is reachable but nothing is listening on `:7734`.
+   Check `docker ps -a` and `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail 100`. If
+   the logs show a Go panic from `regexp.MustCompile`, fix the validator regex,
+   push, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`, then pull/recreate `nas-api` on both NASes.
 
 ### NAS API validator rejecting a command
 
 Run the validator tests with the new command to diagnose the tier:
@@
 `ClassifyTier` returns `-1` for hard-blocked, `1` for read-only, `2` for service
 ops, `3` for file ops. Add a targeted test case rather than guessing at regex
 matches.
+
+If the validator change introduces a new `regexp.MustCompile`, first confirm the
+pattern is valid Go/RE2 syntax. A bad pattern will not merely reject a command; it
+will panic at process start and take the whole NAS API offline.
 
 ### Type errors
*** Update File: /worksp/monitor/app/docs/deployment.md
@@
 No manual steps are needed. Allow up to 5 minutes for the update to propagate after the image push completes.
 
+Because Watchtower recreates the NAS API automatically, a bad image can put both
+NAS API containers into `Exited` or `Restarting` state at nearly the same time.
+`ECONNREFUSED` from the MCP host to `100.107.131.35:7734` and
+`100.107.131.36:7734` means TCP reached the NAS host but no process is listening.
+First check container state and logs, not Tailscale:
+
+```sh
+DOCKER=/var/packages/ContainerManager/target/usr/bin/docker
+$DOCKER ps -a | grep synology-monitor-nas-api
+$DOCKER logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail 100
+```
+
+If the logs show `panic: regexp: Compile(...)`, the NAS API validator contains an
+invalid Go/RE2 regex. Fix the code, push to `main`, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`,
+then pull/recreate only `nas-api`:
+
+```sh
+DOCKER=/var/packages/ContainerManager/target/usr/bin/docker
+cd /volume1/docker/synology-monitor-agent
+$DOCKER compose -f compose.yaml pull nas-api
+$DOCKER compose -f compose.yaml up -d --no-deps nas-api
+curl http://127.0.0.1:7734/health
+```
+
 ### Checking the current version
@@
 The NAS API exposes `GET /health` on port 7734. A `200 OK` response confirms the process is running and the validator loaded:
@@
 These endpoints require no auth. They are reachable from the VPS and any Tailscale-connected machine.
+
+If `/health` returns `ECONNREFUSED`, the NAS-side container is down or not bound to
+`:7734`; this is different from `ETIMEDOUT`, which would indicate network or
+firewall reachability. For `ECONNREFUSED`, inspect `synology-monitor-nas-api`
+container state and logs on each NAS.
 
 ### Watchtower update log
*** Update File: /worksp/monitor/app/docs/architecture.md
@@
 **Tier 1 enforcement:** `writePatterns` (regex list) is checked; any match elevates
 to tier 2+. Real output redirection (`> file`, not `>/dev/null` or `2>&1`) is also
 a write.
+
+The validator is compiled by Go's standard `regexp` package, so every regex must
+be valid RE2 syntax. Lookahead, lookbehind, and backreferences are not supported.
+Do not use patterns such as `(?!...)` in `regexp.MustCompile`; an invalid pattern
+panics at process startup and prevents the NAS API from binding port 7734.
 
 **Tier 2 enforcement:** commands touching `/volume*/` paths outside the monitor
 stack are elevated to tier 3.
*** Update File: /worksp/monitor/app/AGENTS.md
@@
 | Task | Files to touch | Files NOT to touch |
 |---|---|---|
 | Add a NAS MCP tool | `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` (def + `TOOL_GROUPS`), `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` (enable) | `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts` (registry-driven; no registration needed) |
 | Add an agent collector | `apps/agent/internal/collector/<name>.go`, wire in `apps/agent/cmd/agent/main.go` with the `wg.Add(1)` pattern | existing collectors |
 | Send a new agent field to Supabase | `apps/agent/internal/sender/types.go` + a `Queue*` method, **and** a matching column via a new `supabase/migrations/*.sql` | applied migration files |
-| Allow a new NAS command tier | `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` (+ `validator_test.go`) | `executor.go` |
+| Allow a new NAS command tier | `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` (+ `validator_test.go`; Go/RE2 regex only, no lookaround/backrefs) | `executor.go` |
 | Change an AI pipeline stage | `apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage{1,2,3}-*.ts`, `pipeline-v2.ts` | `issue-detector.ts` fingerprinting |
 | Change AI model per stage | Settings UI → `ai_settings` table; fallback chain in `apps/web/src/lib/server/ai-settings.ts` | hardcoded defaults |
 | Add a new log source to the agent | `apps/agent/internal/logwatcher/watcher.go` (`defaultLogFiles`) | No source whitelist to update (migration 00035 dropped it) |
@@
 ### 2026-05 — Claude MCP sessions hanging / failing
 Fix: stateless transport; `Connection: close`; `keepAliveTimeout 120s`.
 Full writeup: [docs/mcp-incident-2026-05.md](docs/mcp-incident-2026-05.md).
+
+### 2026-06 — NAS API crash-loop from invalid Go regexp
+Cause: validator used PCRE-style negative lookahead `(?!...)` inside
+`regexp.MustCompile`; Go RE2 does not support lookaround, so `nas-api` panicked
+at startup after Watchtower pulled the image. Symptom: MCP calls returned
+`ECONNREFUSED` to both NAS `:7734` endpoints. Fix: use RE2-safe positive regexes
+or Go helper code, add validator tests for both match and exception cases, then
+verify `/health` on both NASes after `nas-api-image.yml` publishes.
*** End Patch

 ```sh

+The NAS API validator uses Go's `regexp` package, which is RE2 syntax. Do not use
+PCRE/JavaScript-only features such as lookahead, lookbehind, or backreferences in
+`regexp.MustCompile` patterns. An invalid `MustCompile` pattern panics during
+package initialization and crash-loops `synology-monitor-nas-api` after Watchtower
+pulls the image. If the local machine lacks Go, run the validator tests in Docker:
+
+```sh
+docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
+  go test ./internal/validator/...
+```
+
 ### TypeScript
@@
 Always add a test in `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go`:
 
 ```go
 {name: "my_new_tool tier2", command: "synopkg restart MyPackage", wantTier: 2},
 ```
 
+Regex rule: `apps/nas-api` is Go, so `regexp.MustCompile` patterns must be valid
+Go/RE2 regular expressions. Do not use negative lookahead like `(?!...)` to make
+exceptions. Express the positive match instead, or write a small Go helper and test
+both the matching and exception cases.
+
 Run:
@@
 4. A silent 403 on every request usually means the `NAS_API_SECRET` in Coolify does
    not match `NAS_API_SECRET` in the NAS `.env` — credential parity mismatch.
+5. `ECONNREFUSED` means the NAS is reachable but nothing is listening on `:7734`.

+   the logs show a Go panic from `regexp.MustCompile`, fix the validator regex,
+   push, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`, then pull/recreate `nas-api` on both NASes.
@@

 ops, `3` for file ops. Add a targeted test case rather than guessing at regex
 matches.
+
+If the validator change introduces a new `regexp.MustCompile`, first confirm the
+pattern is valid Go/RE2 syntax. A bad pattern will not merely reject a command; it
+will panic at process start and take the whole NAS API offline.

+The NAS API validator uses Go's `regexp` package, which is RE2 syntax. Do not use
+PCRE/JavaScript-only features such as lookahead, lookbehind, or backreferences in
+`regexp.MustCompile` patterns. An invalid `MustCompile` pattern panics during
+package initialization and crash-loops `synology-monitor-nas-api` after Watchtower
+pulls the image. If the local machine lacks Go, run the validator tests in Docker:
+
+```sh

+## Workflow: adding a new NAS tool
+
+This is the most common extension task. A "NAS tool" is a predefined diagnostic or
+remediation command the MCP server and/or the Stage 2 issue agent can invoke.
+
+### Step 1 — Define the tool in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`
+
+Add an entry to `ALL_TOOL_DEFS`:
+
+```typescript
+{
+  name: "my_new_tool",
+  description: "What it does — be specific, Stage 2 picks tools by description.",
+  write: false,          // true if it modifies NAS state (tier 2/3)
+  params: {
+    target,              // import the shared target param
+    // add any other zod params your command needs
+    filter: z.string().optional().describe("optional search term"),
+  },
+  buildCommand: (input) => [
+    "echo '=== MY TOOL ==='",
+    `grep -r ${quote(String(input.filter ?? ""))} /var/log/my-log.log | tail -n 50`,
+  ].join("\n"),
+},
+```
+
+Add it to `TOOL_GROUPS` in the same file so `tool_search` can find it by group:
+
+```typescript
+const TOOL_GROUPS: Record<string, string> = {
+  // ... existing entries ...
+  my_new_tool: "logs",   // pick the closest group
+};
+```
+
+If you skip `TOOL_GROUPS`, the tool falls into `"misc"` and startup logs a warning.
+It still works, but won't surface in group-based searches.
+
+### Step 2 — Enable the tool in `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`
+
+Add the tool name to `enabled_read_tools` (read-only) or `enabled_write_tools`
+(state-modifying):
 
-pnpm install
-pnpm build      # compiles TypeScript to dist/
-pnpm start      # runs compiled server
+```json
+{
+  "enabled_read_tools": [
+    "...",
+    "my_new_tool"
+  ]
+}
 ```
 
-Tool definitions live in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`. Tool enablement is
-controlled by `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` — changes require a push to `main`
-to deploy.
+A tool present in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but absent from `tools-config.json` is invisible
+to `tool_search` and rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled" message. This lets
+you ship a tool dark and enable it without a code deploy.
 
-The server is stateless (per-request). Do not add session state.
+### Step 3 — For write tools: update the NAS API validator

+Go/RE2 regular expressions. Do not use negative lookahead like `(?!...)` to make
+exceptions. Express the positive match instead, or write a small Go helper and test
+both the matching and exception cases.

+## Workflow: adding a new metric or telemetry type
+
+This is the pipeline for getting a new data source into the issue agent's evidence
+store and prompt.
+
+### Step 1 — Agent collector
+
+Create `apps/agent/internal/collector/yourname.go`. The collector must:
+
+1. Implement a `Run(stop <-chan struct{})` method.
+2. Use the WaitGroup pattern when wired in `main.go`:
+
+   ```go
+   wg.Add(1)
+   go func() {
+       defer wg.Done()
+       yourCollector.Run(stop)
+   }()
+   ```
+
+   Omitting `wg.Add(1)` means graceful shutdown returns before the collector
+   finishes, silently dropping in-flight WAL writes. This exact bug existed in the
+   ShareSync collector (see AGENTS.md §10).
+
+3. Call `sender.Queue*` to buffer rows. Add a payload type to `sender/types.go`
+   and a `Queue*` method to `sender/sender.go` if this is a new table.
+
+4. To persist a cursor between restarts, use `sender.SaveCheckpoint` /
+   `sender.LoadCheckpoint` (writes to the `checkpoints` SQLite table in the WAL).
+
+5. Access host kernel data at `/host/proc/...` and `/host/sys/...` — the NAS
+   compose mounts `/proc:/host/proc:ro` and `/sys:/host/sys:ro`. See `diskstats.go`
+   and `sysextras.go` for examples.
+
+**Do not add a sender payload field without a matching Supabase column.** The
+PostgREST batch will reject the row and trigger poison-row isolation (5 retries,
+then drop).
+
+### Step 2 — Database migration
+
+Add a migration for the new table or column:
 
-To apply locally with the Supabase CLI:
 ```sh
-supabase db push   # apply pending migrations to your local Supabase
+# Next number after 00041:
+supabase/migrations/00042_add_my_new_table.sql
 ```
 
-Applied migrations are permanent — do not edit them. Write a new migration to
-correct a previous one.
+For a new time-series table, follow the pattern in existing migrations for
+pg_partman monthly partitioning. Applied migrations are immutable — never edit
+them.
 
-## Custom metric schedules
+### Step 3 — Supabase sender
 
-To add a new scheduled command (without code changes):
-```sql
-INSERT INTO custom_metric_schedules
-  (name, description, nas_id, collection_command, interval_minutes, is_active, next_run_at)
-VALUES
-  ('my_check', 'What it does', 'edgesynology1', 'cat /proc/loadavg', 60, true, now());
+If the new data belongs in an existing table (e.g. a new `type` in `metrics`), no
+schema change is needed. If it is a new table:
+
+- Add to `upsertTables` in `sender/sender.go` **only** if the table uses
+  merge-on-conflict semantics (like `package_status`). All other tables are
+  append-only inserts.
+- Add the table name to the Go sender payload types in `sender/types.go`.
+
+### Step 4 — `gatherTelemetryContext` in `issue-agent.ts`
+
+Add a Supabase query for the new table in `apps/web/src/lib/server/issue-agent.ts`
+inside the `Promise.all([...])` block:
+
+```typescript
+supabase
+  .from("my_new_table")
+  .select("nas_id, captured_at, my_field, another_field")
+  .gte("captured_at", since6h)
+  .order("captured_at", { ascending: false })
+  .limit(20),
+```
+
+Collect the result:
+
+```typescript
+const myNewData = collectResult("my_new_data", myNewResult, telemetry_errors);
+```
+
+Return it from the function:
+
+```typescript
+return {
+  // ... existing fields ...
+  my_new_data: myNewData,
+};
+```
+
+### Step 5 — Stage 1 `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` in `stage1-structurer.ts`
+
+Add a `SourceMap` entry to the `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` array in
+`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage1-structurer.ts`:
+
+```typescript
+{
+  key: "my_new_data",         // must match the key returned by gatherTelemetryContext
+  source: "my_source_label",  // appears in evidence bodies as "source/severity: body"
+  tsFields: ["captured_at"],  // fields tried in order for the timestamp
+  // bodyFields: ["field1", "field2"], // optional: joined with " — "; omit to use full JSON
+},
 ```
 
-The agent picks it up within 60 seconds and stores output in `custom_metric_data`.
-Stage 2 can read recent output via `fetch_evidence` (source: `custom_metric_data`
-rows surface in the evidence store after the next Stage 1 run).
+**Omit `bodyFields` when diagnostic numbers must be visible.** If you use
+`bodyFields`, only those fields form the evidence body — metric values land in
+`metadata` which `fetch_evidence` never returns, making the data invisible to Stage
+2. See the comment on `top_processes` in the source file.
+
+Stage 1 runs automatically before every Stage 2 turn. Once added here, the new data
+surfaces in `issue_evidence_items` and the bounded evidence slice that Stage 2
+receives in its prompt.
+
+### Step 6 — Stage 2 taxonomy (optional)
+
+If the new data requires interpretation rules (e.g. thresholds, DSM blind spots,
+known false-positive patterns), add them to the `NAS_TAXONOMY` constant in
+`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage2-reasoning.ts`. Keep additions concise — the
+taxonomy is in the stable cacheable prefix, so additions persist across all turns.
+
+### Step 7 — Verify the pipeline
+
+```sh
+pnpm type-check       # catch type mismatches across issue-agent / stage1 / stage2
+```
+
+Check that a new issue run populates `issue_evidence_items` with the expected
+`source` label. The Supabase dashboard or `fetch_evidence` (from Stage 2) can
+confirm rows are landing.
 
 ## Debugging
 
 ### Agent not sending data
-1. Check `/app/data/wal.db` size — if growing, data is queuing but not flushing
-2. Check agent container logs: `docker logs synology-monitor-agent`
-3. Check `nas_logs` freshness in Supabase — if metrics are fresh but logs are stale,
-   one source is probably hitting a bad row; check sender's poison-row isolation log
+
+1. Check `/app/data/wal.db` size — if growing, data is queuing but not flushing.
+2. Check agent container logs: `docker logs synology-monitor-agent`.
+3. Check `nas_logs` and `metrics` freshness in Supabase. If metrics are fresh but
+   logs are stale, one source is hitting a bad row — check the sender log for
+   `[sender] isolating bad row` lines.
+4. An empty table is a bug, not a healthy state. A collector may be hitting an
+   unsupported DSM API — check `nas_logs` for `API unavailable` or error 103 entries.
 
 ### NAS API not responding
-1. Check `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api` on the NAS
-2. Verify Tailscale is connected between the VPS and the NAS
-3. `GET http://<nas-tailscale-ip>:7734/health` should return `{"status":"ok"}`
+
+1. Check `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api` on the NAS.
+2. Verify Tailscale is connected: the web app reaches NAS API over Tailscale IPs
+   (`100.107.131.35:7734` / `100.107.131.36:7734`).
+3. Health check: `GET http://<nas-tailscale-ip>:7734/health` returns `{"status":"ok"}`.
+   This endpoint requires no auth.
+4. A silent 403 on every request usually means the `NAS_API_SECRET` in Coolify does
+   not match `NAS_API_SECRET` in the NAS `.env` — credential parity mismatch.
+5. `ECONNREFUSED` means the NAS is reachable but nothing is listening on `:7734`.
+   Check `docker ps -a` and `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail 100`. If
+   the logs show a Go panic from `regexp.MustCompile`, fix the validator regex,
+   push, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`, then pull/recreate `nas-api` on both NASes.
 
 ### Stage 2 / issue-agent not running
-1. Check `issue_jobs` table — are jobs being enqueued?
-2. Check `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` env var (`inline` runs on-request; `background` needs the worker process)
-3. Check `ai_model_calls` for model errors
+
+1. Check `issue_jobs` table — are jobs being enqueued with `status='pending'`?
+2. Check `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` env var:
+   - `inline` — jobs drain on the next API request to the dashboard
+   - `background` — a separate worker loop drains via `ISSUE_WORKER_TOKEN`
+3. Check `ai_model_calls` table for model errors (rate limits, invalid key, context
+   window exceeded).
+4. Check `issue_stage_runs` for per-stage failure messages.
+
+### Stage 1 evidence missing
+
+If Stage 2 says evidence is empty but data is in the DB:
+
+1. Check `issue_evidence_items` for the issue ID — Stage 1 replaces rows on each
+   run (idempotent delete + insert).
+2. Check the `source` label in `issue_evidence_items` against `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`
+   in `stage1-structurer.ts` — a mismatch in the `key` field silently skips the
+   source.
+3. Check `gatherTelemetryContext` return value — the key must match exactly.
+
+### NAS API validator rejecting a command
+
+Run the validator tests with the new command to diagnose the tier:
+
+```sh
+cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/... -v
+```
+
+`ClassifyTier` returns `-1` for hard-blocked, `1` for read-only, `2` for service
+ops, `3` for file ops. Add a targeted test case rather than guessing at regex
+matches.
+
+If the validator change introduces a new `regexp.MustCompile`, first confirm the
+pattern is valid Go/RE2 syntax. A bad pattern will not merely reject a command; it
+will panic at process start and take the whole NAS API offline.
 
 ### Type errors
+
 ```sh
 pnpm type-check
 ```
-The most common cause is a new DB column added without a matching type annotation,
-or a `fetch()` call without proper response typing.
+
+The most common causes:
+
+- A new DB column added without a matching TypeScript type annotation.
+- A `fetch()` call without proper response typing.
+- A new `gatherTelemetryContext` return field not reflected in `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`.
+- The `shared` package not rebuilt after changing `nas-tools.ts` — run `pnpm build`
+  from the repo root to regenerate `packages/shared/dist/`.
+
+### Build: `guard:ai` CI check failing
+
+The web app has an AI cache guard (`pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai`)
+that enforces stable→dynamic prompt block ordering and normalized provider usage
+fields. If the CI build fails at this step, run the guard locally:
+
+```sh
+cd apps/web && pnpm run guard:ai
+```
+
+The error message identifies which file and which invariant failed.
 
 ## WAL mechanism
 
-If the agent cannot reach Supabase (network outage, Supabase maintenance), all
-telemetry queues in `/app/data/wal.db` on the NAS disk. The WAL is capped at
-`MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB). Once connectivity resumes, the agent flushes
-the backlog automatically. The `checkpoints` table in the same SQLite file stores
-durable collector cursors (log file byte offsets, etc.) so collectors resume from
-the right position after restarts.
+If the agent cannot reach Supabase (network outage, maintenance), all telemetry
+queues in `/app/data/wal.db` on the NAS disk. The WAL is capped at
+`MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB) — oldest entries are dropped at the cap. Once
+connectivity resumes, the agent flushes the backlog automatically.
+
+The `checkpoints` table in the same SQLite file stores durable collector cursors
+(log file byte offsets, ShareSync watermarks, etc.) so collectors resume from the
+right position after restarts.
+
+**Poison-row isolation:** on a PostgREST `4xx`, the sender re-sends each row in
+the failed batch individually. Good rows land; only the bad row accumulates retries
+(max 5, then dropped). This was added after a single bad row silently froze log and
+alert ingestion for 19h and 23 days respectively (see AGENTS.md §13).
+
+## Custom metric schedules
+
+To schedule a recurring shell command without a code change or deploy:
+
+```sql
+INSERT INTO custom_metric_schedules
+  (name, description, nas_id, collection_command, interval_minutes, is_active, next_run_at)
+VALUES
+  ('my_check', 'What it does', 'edgesynology1', 'cat /proc/loadavg', 60, true, now());
+```
+
+The agent polls every 60s, claims due rows with an optimistic lock, and stores
+output in `custom_metric_data`. Stage 2 can read recent output via `fetch_evidence`
+(source: `custom_metric_data` rows surface after the next Stage 1 run for the issue).
+
+Available paths in the agent container:
+
+| Host path | Container path |
+|---|---|
+| `/proc` | `/host/proc` |
+| `/sys` | `/host/sys` |
+| `/var/log` | `/host/log` |
+| `/volume1/@synologydrive` | `/host/shares/@synologydrive` |
+| `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | `/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync` |
+| (other shares) | `/host/shares/<name>` |

payload.message:
Yes. What I did wrong was using a PCRE/JavaScript-style negative lookahead regex in Go code:

`/(?!dev/null)`

Go’s `regexp` package uses RE2, which does not support lookahead/lookbehind/backrefs. Because it was inside `regexp.MustCompile`, the NAS API didn’t just misclassify a command; it panicked during startup and took `:7734` offline on both NASes after Watchtower pulled the image.

I updated the permanent docs to prevent this:

- `AGENTS.md`: added the RE2-only validator rule and a 2026-06 incident note.
- `docs/architecture.md`: documented that NAS API validator regexes must be Go/RE2-safe.
- `docs/deployment.md`: added the `ECONNREFUSED` recovery path and the “panic: regexp” diagnosis.
- `docs/development.md`: added validator test guidance, Docker-based Go test fallback, and the explicit “no lookahead” rule.

I also ran `git diff --check` on those docs; no whitespace errors. Note: `docs/development.md` already had large pre-existing uncommitted edits, so I did not commit anything in this turn to avoid accidentally bundling unrelated work.

Yes. What I did wrong was using a PCRE/JavaScript-style negative lookahead regex in Go code:

`/(?!dev/null)`

Go’s `regexp` package uses RE2, which does not support lookahead/lookbehind/backrefs. Because it was inside `regexp.MustCompile`, the NAS API didn’t just misclassify a command; it panicked during startup and took `:7734` offline on both NASes after Watchtower pulled the image.

I updated the permanent docs to prevent this:

- `AGENTS.md`: added the RE2-only validator rule and a 2026-06 incident note.
- `docs/architecture.md`: documented that NAS API validator regexes must be Go/RE2-safe.
- `docs/deployment.md`: added the `ECONNREFUSED` recovery path and the “panic: regexp” diagnosis.
- `docs/development.md`: added validator test guidance, Docker-based Go test fallback, and the explicit “no lookahead” rule.

I also ran `git diff --check` on those docs; no whitespace errors. Note: `docs/development.md` already had large pre-existing uncommitted edits, so I did not commit anything in this turn to avoid accidentally bundling unrelated work.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
Still needs developer cleanup:

check_scheduled_tasks is looking for /host/usr/syno/etc/schedule/synoscheduler.db, but it reports that DB is not found. It needs updated DSM 7 path discovery.
check_storage_pool_detail tries synoarraystatus, synovolumestatus, and mdadm, but those are unavailable in the agent path/container. It should locate DSM binaries on host paths or gracefully fall back to /proc/mdstat plus smartctl.
Snapshot schedule/retention is still not exposed. ReplicationService package is enabled and synobtrfsreplicad is running, but its package DB/logs did not reveal schedule rules.
So the access expansion helped. The next useful MCP improvement is a dedicated read-only inspect_snapshot_replication tool that discovers DSM’s actual snapshot/retention config paths instead of relying on generic snapshot candidate listing.

payload.message:
Still needs developer cleanup:

check_scheduled_tasks is looking for /host/usr/syno/etc/schedule/synoscheduler.db, but it reports that DB is not found. It needs updated DSM 7 path discovery.
check_storage_pool_detail tries synoarraystatus, synovolumestatus, and mdadm, but those are unavailable in the agent path/container. It should locate DSM binaries on host paths or gracefully fall back to /proc/mdstat plus smartctl.
Snapshot schedule/retention is still not exposed. ReplicationService package is enabled and synobtrfsreplicad is running, but its package DB/logs did not reveal schedule rules.
So the access expansion helped. The next useful MCP improvement is a dedicated read-only inspect_snapshot_replication tool that discovers DSM’s actual snapshot/retention config paths instead of relying on generic snapshot candidate listing.

Got it. I’ll treat these as MCP/NAS tool quality fixes rather than permission problems: update the affected read-only tool definitions to discover DSM 7 paths/binaries more robustly, and improve `inspect_snapshot_replication` so it actively searches Snapshot Replication config/state paths instead of depending on generic listings.

content.text:
the repo for the software running in this folder on this server is: u2giants/synology-monitor [.github](app/.github/) 
read the .md files

you said "The rebuild plan is mostly historical now, but it’s useful because it explains why the current Stage 1/2/3 shape exists". Is there information in the rebuild plan .md file (which should be deleted soon) that's not in the other, persistent .md files?
don't delete plan.md yet but put all the useful information from there into the right place in the correct permanent .md files

another ai session trying to use the NAS-API or synology-monitor MCP server says some tools are not working / blocked. Are all these tools safe to give ai? with no or very low risk of it messing with the file system or kernel or whatever?
Blocked MCP Probes
These were blocked by the NAS API validator:

check_io_stalls
check_io_scheduler
check_psi_pressure
check_storage_pool_detail
check_scrub_status
check_scheduled_tasks
Most returned:

HTTP 403 — command requires tier 2 or higher (detected write pattern)
check_scrub_status returned:

HTTP 403 — docker read command is not in the allowlist
Also, plain read-only compound shell probes were rejected, including commands using awk, egrep, sleep, ps, lsblk, /proc/diskstats, etc., with:

This command requires write access and cannot be run via run_command.
For the developer: the validator is over-classifying read-only diagnostics as write-tier. These tools should either be allowlisted explicitly or split into simpler read-only primitives. The important one to fix first is check_scheduled_tasks, because that would confirm exactly which DSM task is pruning snapshots around :03.
some of Snapshot Replication is visible from the backend, but not cleanly with the current MCP permissions.

I tried just now:

tail_package_logs ReplicationService worked, but it mostly showed package start/stop history, not the active snapshot schedule.
fetch_package_db ReplicationService found no useful DB tables.
list_snapshot_candidates was blocked as “tier 2 / detected write pattern,” even though conceptually it should just list snapshots.
check_package_runtime ReplicationService was also blocked.
So: backend inspection is possible, but the current MCP server is blocking some of the exact read-only paths we would need.

For Snapshot Replication, the backend should ideally expose:

Snapshot schedules per shared folder.
Retention rules per shared folder.
Replication tasks, if any.
Snapshot count per share.
Recent snapshot delete/prune events.
The most useful MCP tool to add/fix would be something like inspect_snapshot_replication, read-only, that gathers those four things.

On md0/md1: your DS1621xs+ is a 6-bay model, so [6/5] [UUUUU_] likely just means DSM created system RAID metadata expecting up to 6 bays, and only 5 disks are currently members. If the unit never had a 6th disk and DSM does not show “System Partition Failed,” there may be nothing to repair. “Repair System Partition” usually appears only when DSM detects a failed/missing system partition on an installed disk. The “fix” would generally be adding a 6th drive and letting DSM initialize/repair the system partition onto it. I would not try to shrink or rewrite md0/md1 manually.

Safe MCP Probes
These blocked probes are definitely low-risk if implemented as narrow read-only allowlisted tools, not as broad tier escalation:

check_psi_pressure

Reads /proc/pressure/*.
Pure read-only. Very safe.
check_io_stalls

Reads ps, /proc/*/wchan, /proc/*/stack, /proc/mdstat, dmesg.
Safe if it does not attach debuggers or run strace.
check_scrub_status

Reads /proc/mdstat and btrfs scrub status.
Safe. Do not include btrfs scrub start/cancel.
check_io_scheduler

Reads /sys/block/*/queue/scheduler, queue settings, maybe iostat -x.
Safe if it never writes to /sys/block/.../scheduler.
check_storage_pool_detail

Reads mdadm --detail, synoarraystatus, synovolumestatus.
Low-risk read-only.
check_scheduled_tasks

Safe if implemented as schedule/list/log inspection only.
Important: do not reuse the same code path as run_scheduled_task, enable_scheduled_task, or disable_scheduled_task.
Also safe and worth unblocking:

list_snapshot_candidates
check_package_runtime
fetch_package_db with SQLite opened read-only, ideally mode=ro
a new inspect_snapshot_replication tool
The key developer guidance: don’t raise the whole NAS API to “tier 2.” Add exact command/tool allowlists for read-only diagnostics. The risky tools are the ones that mutate state: set_io_scheduler, set_vm_dirty_ratios, cancel_btrfs_scrub, start_btrfs_scrub, restart_*, disable_scheduled_task, run_scheduled_task, etc.

the developer that created the synology-monitor MCP server said the below. did you do any of this?:
Both CI builds succeeded. The ff73e58 nas-api image and 9bd7b48 nas-mcp image both built and pushed cleanly. The code changes are not the root cause.

ECONNREFUSED (not ETIMEDOUT) means: Tailscale reachability to both NAS units is fine — TCP reaches the host — but nothing is listening on port 7734. The synology-monitor-nas-api container is down on both machines.

Most likely causes, in order:

Watchtower crash-loop — Watchtower pulled the new nas-api image (~22:53 UTC) and the container now exits immediately on startup. Docker restarts it, it exits again, port 7734 is never bound.
A previous AI session stopped the containers — docker compose stop is a tier-2 allowed command. If an AI session with write tools enabled called it (with approval), the containers stay down because restart: unless-stopped doesn't revive an explicitly-stopped container.
Block device mount failure — docker-compose mounts /dev/sda through /dev/sdh and /dev/md0–md3 by name. If a device listed doesn't exist, Docker refuses to start the container.
Recovery — SSH into each NAS and run these in order:

# 1. Check container state
docker ps -a | grep nas-api

# 2. See why it's failing
docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail=60

# 3a. If it's simply stopped (Exited 0 or 143):
cd /volume1/docker/synology-monitor-agent && docker compose up -d

# 3b. If it's in a crash loop, check env vars are present:
grep -E 'NAS_API_SECRET|NAS_API_APPROVAL_SIGNING_KEY' /volume1/docker/synology-monitor-agent/.env

# 3c. If block device mounts are failing, edit docker-compose.agent.yml
#     and comment out bays that don't have drives installed
If step 3a works and port 7734 comes back up, run the same on edgesynology2.

payload.message: The three weak spots are exactly where expected: one hard-coded scheduler DB path, storage pool commands assuming DSM binaries are in one place, and Snapshot Replication doing generic DB/log sampling. I’m going to ask the NASes read-only where DSM 7 actually keeps these bits, then encode that discovery into the MCP tool instead of relying on this one environment.

{"cmd":"DEVOPS_TOKEN='[REDACTED]'
cmd='ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new edgesynology1 '\''printf "=== schedule candidates ===\n"; find /usr/syno/etc /etc /var /var/packages /volume1/@appdata /volume1/@database -maxdepth 6 \( -iname "*scheduler*.db" -o -iname "*synoscheduler*" -o -iname "*schedule*" \) 2>/dev/null | head -80; printf "\n=== storage binaries ===\n"; for b in synoarraystatus synovolumestatus mdadm synostorage synodisk syno_disk_ctl smartctl; do printf "-- %s --\n" "$b"; command -v "$b" 2>/dev/null; find /usr/syno /usr /sbin /bin -maxdepth 4 -type f -name "$b" 2>/dev/null | head -10; done; printf "\n=== snapshot candidates ===\n"; find /usr/syno/etc /etc /var/packages/SnapshotReplication /var/packages/ReplicationService /volume1/@appdata /volume1/@database /volume1/@appconf -maxdepth 7 \( -iname "*.db" -o -iname "*.sqlite" -o -iname "*.conf" -o -iname "*.json" -o -iname "*snapshot*" -o -iname "*replica*" \) 2>/dev/null | head -160'\'''
json=$(jq -n --arg cmd "$cmd" '{jsonrpc:"2.0",id:1,method:"tools/call",params:{name:"run_command",arguments:{command:$cmd,timeout:120}}}')
curl -fsS https://mcp.designflow.app/mcp -H "Authorization: [REDACTED] [REDACTED_AUTH]" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' -d "$json" | awk '/^data: /{sub(/^data: /,""); print}' | jq -r '.result.content[]?.text // (.result.structuredContent|tostring) // .'","workdir":"/worksp/monitor/app","yield_time_ms":30000,"max_output_tokens":20000}

+	      "echo '=== SYSFS MD STATE FALLBACK ==='",
+	      "for mdpath in /sys/block/md*/md; do",
+	      "  [ -d \"$mdpath\" ] || continue",
+	      "  echo \"--- ${mdpath%/md} ---\"",
+	      "  for f in array_state level raid_disks degraded sync_action sync_completed mismatch_cnt metadata_version; do",
+	      "    [ -r \"$mdpath/$f\" ] && printf '%s=%s\\n' \"$f\" \"$(cat \"$mdpath/$f\" 2>/dev/null)\"",
+	      "  done",
+	      "  echo ''",
+	      "done 2>/dev/null || echo 'No sysfs MD state files found'",
+	      "echo ''",

-	      "for d in /dev/sd?; do [ -b \"$d\" ] || continue; echo \"--- $d ---\"; smartctl -i \"$d\" 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'Device Model|Model Number|Serial|Capacity' | head -3; done 2>/dev/null || echo 'smartctl not available'",
+	      "smart_bin=$(find_tool smartctl || true)",
+	      "if [ -z \"$smart_bin\" ]; then echo 'smartctl not found'; fi",
+	      "for d in /dev/sd? /dev/nvme?n?; do",
+	      "  [ -b \"$d\" ] || continue",
+	      "  echo \"--- $d ---\"",
+	      "  if [ -n \"$smart_bin\" ]; then",
+	      "    \"$smart_bin\" -i \"$d\" 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'Device Model|Model Number|Serial|Capacity|User Capacity|Firmware' | head -6",
+	      "    \"$smart_bin\" -H \"$d\" 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'result|Status' || true",
+	      "  else",
+	      "    echo 'smartctl unavailable'",
+	      "  fi",
+	      "done 2>/dev/null || true",
 	    ].join("\n"),
 	  },
*** End Patch

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /worksp/monitor/app/packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts
@@
-	        "echo '=== SNAPSHOT REPLICATION DATABASES ==='",
-	        "dbs=$(find /host/var/packages/SnapshotReplication/var /var/packages/SnapshotReplication/var /volume[0-9]*/@appdata/SnapshotReplication -maxdepth 6 \\( -name '*.db' -o -name '*.sqlite' \\) 2>/dev/null | head -10)",
-	        "if [ -z \"$dbs\" ]; then echo 'No SnapshotReplication SQLite DBs found in known locations.'; fi",
+	        "echo '=== SNAPSHOT REPLICATION CONFIG PATH DISCOVERY ==='",
+	        "snapshot_roots='/host/usr/syno/etc /host/usr/syno/etc/packages /host/etc /host/var/packages/SnapshotReplication /host/var/packages/ReplicationService /var/packages/SnapshotReplication /var/packages/ReplicationService /volume1/@appdata /volume2/@appdata /volume1/@database /volume2/@database /volume1/@appconf /volume2/@appconf /volume1/@appstore/SnapshotReplication /volume2/@appstore/SnapshotReplication /volume1/@appstore/ReplicationService /volume2/@appstore/ReplicationService'",
+	        "echo 'Roots present:'",
+	        "for root in $snapshot_roots; do [ -e \"$root\" ] && echo \"  $root\"; done",
+	        "cfgs=$(",
+	        "  for root in $snapshot_roots; do",
+	        "    [ -d \"$root\" ] || continue",
+	        "    find \"$root\" -maxdepth 6 \\( -iname '*.conf' -o -iname '*.json' -o -iname '*.ini' -o -iname '*.cfg' -o -iname '*.db' -o -iname '*.sqlite' \\) 2>/dev/null",
+	        "  done | grep -Ei 'snapshot|replica|retent|sched|task|btrfs|share|folder|rotate|policy' | sort -u | head -80",
+	        ")",
+	        "if [ -z \"$cfgs\" ]; then echo 'No candidate Snapshot Replication config/DB files found in known DSM 7 roots.'; else echo \"$cfgs\"; fi",
+	        "echo ''",
+	        "echo '=== TEXT CONFIG SCHEDULE / RETENTION CLUES ==='",
+	        "text_cfgs=$(echo \"$cfgs\" | grep -Eiv '\\.(db|sqlite)$' | head -30)",
+	        "if [ -z \"$text_cfgs\" ]; then echo 'No candidate text config files found.'; fi",
+	        "for f in $text_cfgs; do",
+	        "  [ -f \"$f\" ] || continue",
+	        "  echo \"--- $f ---\"",
+	        `  grep -I -iE 'schedule|retention|rotate|rotation|policy|snapshot|replica|share|folder|keep|hour|daily|weekly|monthly|task|prune|delete|remove' "$f" 2>/dev/null${grepFilter} | head -80 || true`,
+	        "done",
+	        "echo ''",
+	        "echo '=== SNAPSHOT REPLICATION DATABASES ==='",
+	        "dbs=$(echo \"$cfgs\" | grep -Ei '\\.(db|sqlite)$' | head -20)",
+	        "if [ -z \"$dbs\" ]; then echo 'No Snapshot Replication SQLite DBs found in discovered config paths.'; fi",
 	        "for dbfile in $dbs; do",
 	        "  echo \"--- DB: $dbfile ---\"",
 	        "  echo 'tables:'",
 	        "  timeout 8 sqlite3 -readonly \"$dbfile\" '.tables' 2>&1 | head -20",
@@
 	        "  for tbl in $tables; do",
 	        "    echo \"--- table: $tbl schema ---\"",
 	        "    timeout 8 sqlite3 -readonly \"$dbfile\" \".schema \\\"$tbl\\\"\" 2>&1 | head -20",
+	        "    echo \"--- table: $tbl columns ---\"",
+	        "    timeout 8 sqlite3 -readonly \"$dbfile\" \"PRAGMA table_info(\\\"$tbl\\\");\" 2>&1 | head -40",
 	        "    echo \"--- table: $tbl sample rows ---\"",
 	        "    timeout 8 sqlite3 -readonly \"$dbfile\" \"SELECT * FROM \\\"$tbl\\\" LIMIT 10;\" 2>&1 | head -40",
 	        "  done",
*** End Patch

+      "mdadm_bin=$(find_tool mdadm || true)",
+      "if [ -z \"$mdadm_bin\" ]; then echo 'mdadm not found in host/container candidate paths'; fi",

       "echo ''",

       "echo ''",
+      "echo '=== SYSFS MD STATE FALLBACK ==='",
+      "for mdpath in /sys/block/md*/md; do",
+      "  [ -d \"$mdpath\" ] || continue",
+      "  echo \"--- ${mdpath%/md} ---\"",
+      "  for f in array_state level raid_disks degraded sync_action sync_completed mismatch_cnt metadata_version; do",
+      "    [ -r \"$mdpath/$f\" ] && printf '%s=%s\\n' \"$f\" \"$(cat \"$mdpath/$f\" 2>/dev/null)\"",

+      "  echo ''",
+      "done 2>/dev/null || echo 'No sysfs MD state files found'",

+      "for d in /dev/sd? /dev/nvme?n?; do",
+      "  [ -b \"$d\" ] || continue",
+      "  echo \"--- $d ---\"",
+      "  if [ -n \"$smart_bin\" ]; then",
+      "    \"$smart_bin\" -i \"$d\" 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'Device Model|Model Number|Serial|Capacity|User Capacity|Firmware' | head -6",
+      "    \"$smart_bin\" -H \"$d\" 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'result|Status' || true",
+      "  else",
+      "    echo 'smartctl unavailable'",
+      "  fi",
+      "done 2>/dev/null || true",
     ].join("\n"),
   },
*** End Patch

payload.message:
Done and pushed.

Changed [packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts](/worksp/monitor/app/packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts:924):

- `check_scheduled_tasks` now does DSM 7 path discovery, opens SQLite with `-readonly`, reports checked roots, and scans discovered scheduler logs.
- `check_storage_pool_detail` now discovers DSM/host binaries from explicit read-only paths and falls back to `/proc/mdstat`, `/sys/block/md*/md`, and `smartctl`.
- `inspect_snapshot_replication` now includes daemon/runtime discovery, binary discovery, package-specific DSM 7 config/DB roots, text config schedule/retention clues, and read-only SQLite schema/row sampling.

Committed and pushed:

`2ad8f52 nas-mcp: improve DSM 7 read-only discovery probes`

Verified:

- `pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/shared build`
- `pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/nas-mcp build`
- `git diff --check`
- GitHub Actions passed:
  - `Publish NAS MCP Image`
  - `Publish Web Image`
- Deployed MCP health check returns OK with `registry_tools: 118`.

Only remaining local change is the pre-existing/generated `apps/web/tsconfig.tsbuildinfo`, left uncommitted.

content.text:
Done and pushed.

Changed [packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts](/worksp/monitor/app/packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts:924):

- `check_scheduled_tasks` now does DSM 7 path discovery, opens SQLite with `-readonly`, reports checked roots, and scans discovered scheduler logs.
- `check_storage_pool_detail` now discovers DSM/host binaries from explicit read-only paths and falls back to `/proc/mdstat`, `/sys/block/md*/md`, and `smartctl`.
- `inspect_snapshot_replication` now includes daemon/runtime discovery, binary discovery, package-specific DSM 7 config/DB roots, text config schedule/retention clues, and read-only SQLite schema/row sampling.

Committed and pushed:

`2ad8f52 nas-mcp: improve DSM 7 read-only discovery probes`

Verified:

- `pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/shared build`
- `pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/nas-mcp build`
- `git diff --check`
- GitHub Actions passed:
  - `Publish NAS MCP Image`
  - `Publish Web Image`
- Deployed MCP health check returns OK with `registry_tools: 118`.

Only remaining local change is the pre-existing/generated `apps/web/tsconfig.tsbuildinfo`, left uncommitted.

- `check_scheduled_tasks` now does DSM 7 path discovery, opens SQLite with `-readonly`, reports checked roots, and scans discovered scheduler logs.
- `check_storage_pool_detail` now discovers DSM/host binaries from explicit read-only paths and falls back to `/proc/mdstat`, `/sys/block/md*/md`, and `smartctl`.
- `inspect_snapshot_replication` now includes daemon/runtime discovery, binary discovery, package-specific DSM 7 config/DB roots, text config schedule/retention clues, and read-only SQLite schema/row sampling.

Committed and pushed:

`2ad8f52 nas-mcp: improve DSM 7 read-only discovery probes`

Verified:

- `pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/shared build`
- `pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/nas-mcp build`

payload.message: i'm not a programmer, so i don't understand: why uncommitted?

“Uncommitted” just means there is a file on this machine that differs from the last saved Git version, but I did not include it in the Git commit I pushed.

In this case it’s:

`/worksp/monitor/app/apps/web/tsconfig.tsbuildinfo`

That file is a generated TypeScript build cache file. Build tools often update it automatically when we run builds. It is usually not meaningful source code, and committing it can create noisy changes. So I left it alone instead of pushing random build-cache churn.

The actual work you asked for was committed and pushed successfully.

content.text:
On edgesynology2, it still does not find the scheduler DB. It only checked:

/host/etc
So the developer fixed discovery for one NAS path, but edgesynology2 still needs host path coverage similar to edgesynology1.
Still missing:

Snapshot schedule/retention rules are not exposed yet.
list_snapshot_candidates still finds no visible snapshots.
ReplicationService DB still has no useful DB files/tables.
Developer next fix: make check_scheduled_tasks find the scheduler DB on edgesynology2, then add a read-only Snapshot Replication inspector that parses the actual SnapshotReplication schedule/retention config.

payload.message:

/host/etc

Developer next fix: make check_scheduled_tasks find the scheduler DB on edgesynology2, then add a read-only Snapshot Replication inspector that parses the actual SnapshotReplication schedule/retention config.

Got it. I’m going back into the actual deployed environment for `edgesynology2` first, because “only checked `/host/etc`” means this is probably a mount/layout mismatch, not just a missing filename pattern. Then I’ll patch the tool to cover that layout cleanly.

payload.message: The repo is clean except for that generated web cache file. I’m probing `edgesynology2` with short, targeted SSH commands now: mount points, whether `/host/usr` exists inside the agent, and exact scheduler/snapshot package directories.

content.text: The repo is clean except for that generated web cache file. I’m probing `edgesynology2` with short, targeted SSH commands now: mount points, whether `/host/usr` exists inside the agent, and exact scheduler/snapshot package directories.

payload.name: exec_command
payload.arguments:
{"cmd":"DEVOPS_TOKEN='[REDACTED]'
cmd='ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new edgesynology2 '\''set -u; echo === host root dirs ===; ls -ld /usr/syno/etc /etc /var/packages /volume1/@appstore/SnapshotReplication /volume1/@appdata/SnapshotReplication /volume1/@appconf/SnapshotReplication 2>/dev/null || true; echo === schedule files exact ===; for p in /usr/syno/etc/schedule /usr/syno/etc/synotask /etc /var/services /var/packages; do [ -e "$p" ] && { echo -- $p; find "$p" -maxdepth 4 \( -iname "*scheduler*.db" -o -iname "*synoscheduler*.db" -o -iname "*task*.db" -o -iname "*schedule*.db" \) 2>/dev/null | head -40; }; done; echo === snapshot package files ===; for p in /var/packages/SnapshotReplication /var/packages/ReplicationService /volume1/@appdata/SnapshotReplication /volume1/@appconf/SnapshotReplication /volume1/@appstore/SnapshotReplication; do [ -e "$p" ] && { echo -- $p; find "$p" -maxdepth 5 \( -iname "*.db" -o -iname "*.sqlite" -o -iname "*.conf" -o -iname "*.json" -o -iname "*.ini" -o -iname "*.cfg" \) 2>/dev/null | head -80; }; done'\'''
json=$(jq -n --arg cmd "$cmd" '{jsonrpc:"2.0",id:1,method:"tools/call",params:{name:"run_command",arguments:{command:$cmd,timeout:45}}}')
curl -fsS https://mcp.designflow.app/mcp -H "Authorization: [REDACTED] [REDACTED_AUTH]" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' -d "$json" | awk '/^data: /{sub(/^data: /,""); print}' | jq -r '.result.content[]?.text // (.result.structuredContent|tostring) // .'","workdir":"/worksp/monitor/app","yield_time_ms":50000,"max_output_tokens":20000}

payload.message: The live SSH command timed out without output, so I’m not going to spend cycles fighting that path. The safer fix is to make the MCP probe resilient inside the NAS API container: check `/host`, `/hostfs`, `/mnt/host`, and unprefixed DSM mounts, and add read-only DSM WebAPI listing as a fallback when the DB path isn’t mounted.

payload.message: I found the structural issue: the checked roots are hard-coded to `/host/...`, but the agent/container setup may only expose a subset of `/host` on one NAS. I’m adding a small shell path resolver so the same probe checks `/host`, `/hostfs`, `/mnt/host`, and unprefixed DSM paths without turning into a broad scan.

content.text: I found the structural issue: the checked roots are hard-coded to `/host/...`, but the agent/container setup may only expose a subset of `/host` on one NAS. I’m adding a small shell path resolver so the same probe checks `/host`, `/hostfs`, `/mnt/host`, and unprefixed DSM paths without turning into a broad scan.

services:
  synology-monitor-agent:
    image: ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-agent:${AGENT_IMAGE_TAG:-latest}
    container_name: synology-monitor-agent
    network_mode: host
    restart: unless-stopped
    env_file:
      - .env
    environment:
      TZ: ${TZ:-UTC}
    volumes:
      - /proc:/host/proc:ro          # per-process CPU/mem/IO and disk stats
      - /sys:/host/sys:ro            # cgroup IO stats, btrfs counters, thermal data
      - /etc/passwd:[REDACTED]
      - /var/log:/host/log:ro
      - /var/packages:/host/packages:ro
      # Synology Container Manager rejects top-level volume binds such as
      # /volume1 during compose/UI recreates. Mount explicit shared folders.
      # Set SHARED_*_PATH env vars or use defaults.
      - ${SHARE_FILES_PATH:-/volume1/files}:/host/shares/files:ro
      - ${SHARE_STYLEGUIDES_PATH:-/volume1/styleguides}:/host/shares/styleguides:ro
      - ${SHARE_USERS_PATH:-/volume1/users}:/host/shares/users:ro
      - ${SHARE_HOMES_PATH:-/volume1/homes}:/host/shares/homes:ro
      - ${SHARE_COLDLION_PATH:-/volume1/Coldlion}:/host/shares/Coldlion:ro

      - ${SHARE_MGMT_PATH:-/volume1/mgmt}:/host/shares/mgmt:ro
      - ${SHARE_MAC_PATH:-/volume1/mac}:/host/shares/mac:ro
      - ${SHARE_OLDSTYLEGUIDES_PATH:-/volume1/oldStyleguides}:/host/shares/oldStyleguides:ro
      # System folders for ShareSync monitoring
      - ${SHARE_SYNO_DRIVE_PATH:-/volume1/@synologydrive}:/host/shares/@synologydrive:ro
      - ${SHARE_SYNO_DRIVE_SYNC_PATH:-/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync}:/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync:ro
      - ${HYPERBACKUP_APPDATA_PATH:-/volume1/@appdata/HyperBackup}:/host/appdata/HyperBackup:ro
      - agent-data:/app/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "test", "-f", "/app/data/wal.db"]
      interval: 60s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 45s
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"

  # NAS API — three-tier HTTP shell execution service used by the issue agent.
  # The web app (running in the cloud) calls this to investigate and remediate issues.
  # Tier 1 commands auto-execute; Tier 2/3 require an HMAC-signed approval token.
  nas-api:
    image: ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-api:${AGENT_IMAGE_TAG:-latest}
    container_name: synology-monitor-nas-api
    network_mode: host         # must see the same network interfaces as the NAS
    pid: host                  # share host PID namespace so pkill/ps can reach DSM processes
    restart: unless-stopped
    security_opt:
      - "apparmor=unconfined"  # Synology DSM rejects default apparmor profile on container init
    cap_add:
      - SYS_ADMIN              # required for btrfs subvolume list, scrub, and snapshot operations
      - SYS_PTRACE             # strace on D-state/hung processes — code-injection vectors (gdb, lldb) are hard-blocked in the NAS-API validator
    env_file:
      - .env
    environment:
      TZ: ${TZ:-UTC}
    volumes:
      - /proc:/host/proc:ro    # mounted as /host/proc to avoid shadowing container's own /proc
      - /sys:/host/sys:ro      # same pattern: keep container /sys intact for apparmor init
      - /usr/syno:/host/usr/syno:ro
      # Host system libraries — required for glibc Synology binaries (synopkg, synoacltool, etc.)
      # Tools call them with LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/host/lib:/host/usr/lib:/host/usr/syno/lib
      - /lib:/host/lib:ro
      - /usr/lib:/host/usr/lib:ro
      - /etc/passwd:[REDACTED]
      - /etc/VERSION:/host/etc/VERSION:ro
      - /etc/synoinfo.conf:/host/etc/synoinfo.conf:ro
      - /etc.defaults:/host/etc.defaults:ro
      - /var/log:/host/log:ro
      - /var/packages:/host/packages:ro
      - /var/packages/Tailscale:/var/packages/Tailscale:ro
      # Full btrfs volume mount — required for btrfs subvolume list, scrub, and snapshot ops.
      # CAP_SYS_ADMIN (above) is also required. Defaults to /volume1.
      # For a second volume set BTRFS_VOLUME2_PATH=/volume2 and uncomment the next line.
      - ${BTRFS_VOLUME1_PATH:-/volume1}:/btrfs/volume1:rw
      # - ${BTRFS_VOLUME2_PATH:-/volume2}:/btrfs/volume2:rw
      - ${SHARE_FILES_PATH:-/volume1/files}:/volume1/files:ro
      - ${SHARE_STYLEGUIDES_PATH:-/volume1/styleguides}:/volume1/styleguides:ro
      - ${SHARE_USERS_PATH:-/volume1/users}:/volume1/users:ro
      - ${SHARE_HOMES_PATH:-/volume1/homes}:/volume1/homes:ro
      - ${SHARE_COLDLION_PATH:-/volume1/Coldlion}:/volume1/Coldlion:ro
      - ${SHARE_PHOTOGRAPHY_PATH:-/volume1/Photography}:/volume1/Photography:ro
      - ${SHARE_FREELANCERS_PATH:-/volume1/freelancers}:/volume1/freelancers:ro
      - ${SHARE_MGMT_PATH:-/volume1/mgmt}:/volume1/mgmt:ro
      - ${SHARE_MAC_PATH:-/volume1/mac}:/volume1/mac:ro
      - ${SHARE_OLDSTYLEGUIDES_PATH:-/volume1/oldStyleguides}:/volume1/oldStyleguides:ro
      - ${SHARE_SYNO_DRIVE_PATH:-/volume1/@synologydrive}:/volume1/@synologydrive:ro
      - ${SHARE_SYNO_DRIVE_SYNC_PATH:-/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync}:/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync:ro
      - ${HYPERBACKUP_APPDATA_PATH:-/volume1/@appdata/HyperBackup}:/volume1/@appdata/HyperBackup:ro
      - ${MONITOR_STACK_PATH:-/volume1/docker/synology-monitor-agent}:/volume1/docker/synology-monitor-agent:ro
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      # Block devices (read-only) — required for hdparm -I (device identity/features) and
      # hdparm -t (throughput test). Individual named mounts only — NOT the full /dev tree.
      # Docker bind-mounts fail if the source device doesn't exist. Comment out empty bays.
      # Typical Synology layout: sda–sdh are drive bays; md0–md3 are RAID arrays.
      - /dev/sda:/dev/sda:ro
      - /dev/sdb:/dev/sdb:ro
      - /dev/sdc:/dev/sdc:ro
      - /dev/sdd:/dev/sdd:ro
      - /dev/sde:/dev/sde:ro
      - /dev/sdf:/dev/sdf:ro
      - /dev/sdg:/dev/sdg:ro
      - /dev/sdh:/dev/sdh:ro
      - /dev/md0:/dev/md0:ro
      - /dev/md1:/dev/md1:ro
      - /dev/md2:/dev/md2:ro
      - /dev/md3:/dev/md3:ro
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:${NAS_API_PORT:-7734}/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 10s
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "5m"
        max-file: "3"

  # Watchtower polls GHCR every 5 minutes and restarts updated containers
  # automatically when new images are available.
  watchtower:
    image: containrrr/watchtower
    container_name: synology-monitor-watchtower
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
    environment:
      TZ: ${TZ:-UTC}
      WATCHTOWER_POLL_INTERVAL: "300"
      WATCHTOWER_CLEANUP: "true"
      WATCHTOWER_NO_STARTUP_MESSAGE: "true"
    command: synology-monitor-agent synology-monitor-nas-api

volumes:
  agent-data:

payload.message: There’s also a second concrete bug: `list_snapshot_candidates` was mostly looking under `/volumeN`, but the compose file mounts full Btrfs volumes at `/btrfs/volumeN`. I’m fixing that too, so snapshot listing uses the path that actually has the full volume mount.

payload.message: Snapshot listing now checks `/btrfs/volumeN`, and Snapshot Replication roots include the actual compose mount `/host/packages`. I’m adding a read-only WebAPI discovery section for Snapshot Replication APIs so the inspector can expose schedules/retention even when the package stores them behind DSM APIs rather than obvious DB files.

payload.message:
Fixed and pushed.

New commit:

`93b82b2 nas-mcp: widen read-only DSM discovery paths`

What changed:

- `check_scheduled_tasks` now checks the real compose-mounted package path `/host/packages` as well as `/host/usr/syno`, unprefixed DSM paths, and logs.
- It also has a read-only DSM WebAPI fallback using `SYNO.Core.TaskScheduler.list`, so it can still list scheduled tasks even if the SQLite DB is not mounted in the container.
- `list_snapshot_candidates` now checks `/btrfs/volumeN`, which is where the full Btrfs volume is actually mounted for the NAS API container.
- `inspect_snapshot_replication` now checks `/btrfs/volumeN`, `/host/packages/SnapshotReplication`, `/host/packages/ReplicationService`, and adds read-only DSM WebAPI discovery for Snapshot/Replication/Btrfs APIs.

Verified:

- Shared package build passed.
- NAS MCP build passed.
- NAS API validator tests passed in Docker.
- GitHub Actions passed:
  - `Publish NAS MCP Image`
  - `Publish Web Image`
- Coolify redeploy triggered.
- Public MCP health endpoint is OK.

Still left uncommitted: only the generated `apps/web/tsconfig.tsbuildinfo` cache file.

Documentation is part of the work, not a final cleanup step.

Update Markdown documentation to reflect the actual current state of the codebase. Derive everything from the code, config, scripts, workflows, deployment files, and changes made in this session. Do not invent architecture, intent, or future plans.

## Primary rule

`AGENTS.md` is the canonical guide for future developers and AI coding sessions.

A new senior engineer or AI session should be able to read `AGENTS.md` in under 5 minutes and understand:

- what the project does
- where custom code lives
- where important files are
- what not to touch
- how local development works
- how deployment works
- what is incomplete
- what intentional quirks must not be undone

Keep other docs focused and avoid duplicating the same information everywhere.

## Required documentation files

Use these roles:

- `README.md` — entry point and quick orientation
- `AGENTS.md` — primary AI/developer operating guide
- `CLAUDE.md` — Claude Code-specific notes only; point to `AGENTS.md`
- `docs/architecture.md` — system design, major components, data flow, constraints
- `docs/development.md` — local setup, run/test/lint/debug workflow
- `docs/configuration.md` — environment variables, config files, feature flags
- `docs/deployment.md` — deploy/release/environment workflow
- folder-level `README.md` — only where a subfolder genuinely needs local context

Create missing docs when useful. Remove or fix stale, misleading, redundant, or vague docs.

## Required `AGENTS.md` content

Maintain these sections in `AGENTS.md`:

### 1. Project summary

One short business/technical summary explaining what this project does, who uses it, the key moving parts, and what outcome matters.

### 2. Multi-model AI note

Include this near the top:

```markdown
There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.

`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.

When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.
3. Repository structure
Explain the important directories and separate:
•	code we own 
•	generated code 
•	third-party/vendor/framework code 
•	build artifacts 
•	docs 
•	scripts 
•	migrations 
•	deployment files 
4. Prime Directive: custom-code boundary
Explicitly list where project-owned custom code belongs.
Use this format and adapt it to the actual repo:
Our custom code lives here:

- `src/modules/[project-name]/`
- `src/app/[project-owned-routes]/`
- `docs/`
- `.github/workflows/`

Everything else requires justification before touching.
The purpose is to prevent AI agents from scattering project logic across unrelated framework/vendor files.
5. Core modification inventory
If any files outside project-owned areas were modified, list them.
| File | Change made | Why it was necessary | Risk during upgrades |
|---|---|---|---|
| `path/to/file` | Short description | Specific reason | What to check later |
Keep the section even if empty.
6. Task-to-file navigation
Give concrete guidance for common changes.
| Task | Files to touch | Files not to touch |
|---|---|---|
| Change login screen | `src/...` | `vendor/...` |
| Add database field | `db/migrations/...`, `src/models/...` | existing applied migrations |
| Add new setting | `src/config/...`, `docs/configuration.md` | production `.env` directly |
7. Data model and external identifiers
Document important entities, fields, identifiers, UUIDs, container names, project IDs, webhook names, deployment targets, and external-system IDs.
| Entity/System | Identifier | Where defined | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer | `customer_id` | database | UUID |
| Coolify app | `[uuid]` | Coolify | production deploy target |
| Supabase project | `[project-id]` | Supabase | database backend |
Do not casually rename or regenerate documented identifiers.
8. Container and service inventory
List every relevant runtime service/container.
| Container/service | Purpose | Managed by | App/project ID | Image/source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `app-web` | frontend web app | Coolify | `[uuid]` | `ghcr.io/...` |
| `app-db` | database | Compose/Coolify | `[uuid]` | postgres |
9. What to ignore
List files/directories that exist but are not relevant to active development, such as:
•	node_modules/ 
•	dist/ 
•	.cache/ 
•	coverage/ 
•	generated SDKs 
•	vendor examples 
•	third-party docs 
10. Intentional quirks and non-obvious decisions
Document anything that looks wrong, unusual, duplicated, overcomplicated, or counterintuitive but is intentional.
Use this format:
### [Decision name]

Looks like:
[How a new developer or AI session might misread it.]

Actually:
[What it really is.]

Why:
[The constraint, incident, requirement, or tradeoff.]

Do not change because:
[What would break or become worse.]
11. Credentials and environment
List required variables without secret values.
| Variable | Purpose | Stored where | Required in dev | Required in prod |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DATABASE_URL` | database connection | Coolify env | yes | yes |
| `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` | AI model calls | Coolify env | yes | yes |
12. Deployment
Document the real deployment path, including:
•	GitHub Actions workflow name 
•	image/package names 
•	tag pattern 
•	deployment platform/app/project ID 
•	deploy trigger method 
•	rollback method 
•	where runtime environment variables live 
•	whether SSH is allowed, and whether it is routine or exceptional 
13. Critical incidents
Document disasters, near-misses, migrations, broken deploys, data loss risks, or important recovery lessons.
### [YYYY-MM-DD] [Incident title]

What happened:
...

Impact:
...

Root cause:
...

Recovery:
...

Rule added to prevent recurrence:
...
Keep the section even if empty.
14. Pending work
Keep this current.
| Status | Item | Owner/next action |
|---|---|---|
| open | Add email sending | Needs SMTP credentials |
| blocked | Deploy production | Waiting for Coolify app ID |
| done | Initial Dockerfile | Completed in commit `[sha]` |
A stale pending-work section is harmful. Update it or remove completed items.
CLAUDE.md
Keep CLAUDE.md short. It should not duplicate AGENTS.md.
Include only:
•	“Read AGENTS.md first” 
•	Claude Code-specific memory/context notes 
•	.claudeignore guidance 
•	allowed operations/tools/APIs 
•	SSH permissions, if any 
•	explicit note that SSH is not the normal deployment path unless that is actually true 
•	commit style preferences 
•	Claude-specific behaviors to enable or suppress 
Ignore files
Create or update .claudeignore and .cursorignore with matching content where appropriate.
Standard entries:
dist/
node_modules/
.cache/
coverage/
Add generated, third-party, vendor, build, or cache directories that should not consume AI context.
Create .copilotignore too if this repo uses GitHub Copilot and the ignore rules matter there.
Documentation quality rules
•	Base docs on actual code and actual changes. 
•	Be accurate, specific, and high-signal. 
•	No generic filler. 
•	Put information in the most appropriate file. 
•	Do not dump everything into README.md. 
•	Do not duplicate the same guidance across files. 
•	Explain intentional quirks that future developers or AI agents may mistake for bugs. 
•	Fix stale or misleading docs as part of the task. 
•	If a code change affects docs, update docs in the same commit. 
•	If the change does not affect docs, say so explicitly. 
Handoff rule
If any work is unfinished or in progress, create HANDOFF.md in the repo root.
HANDOFF.md must be detailed enough that a developer who has never seen this codebase can continue without asking questions.
Include:
•	what was being built or fixed and why 
•	what is fully done 
•	what is partially done and its exact state 
•	what has not been started 
•	decisions made during this session and why 
•	dead ends or abandoned approaches 
•	exact next action 
•	known risks, blockers, or unknowns 
•	any context from this session that would otherwise be lost 
Delete HANDOFF.md once the work it describes is complete.
Completion report
When finished, commit and push documentation changes to github.com with a clear commit message.
Then summarize:
•	which documentation files changed 
•	what changed in each 
•	whether HANDOFF.md exists and why 
•	any remaining documentation gaps
